SUMMARY:
In 2023, we saw the first approval of a CRISPR-based gene-editing therapy. And many more are to come. So let's take a look at the developments that made news

FULL CONTENT:
# Gene editing had a banner year in 2023

This year, gene editing finally started living up to its potential

![an altered strand of DNA spotlight on a purple stage with theatrical curtains](https://wp.technologyreview.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/gene-therapy.jpg)

*This article first appeared in The Checkup, MIT Technology Review's weekly biotech newsletter. To receive it in your inbox every Thursday, and read articles like this first, [sign up here](https://forms.technologyreview.com/newsletters/biotech-the-checkup/?_ga=2.241810882.15113993.1664981064-43237434.1647441349).*

Welcome back to The Checkup. This will be our last issue of 2023, so this week I’ve been reflecting on our biotechnology coverage over the past year. As I scrolled through our archives, I was struck by the vast number of stories we wrote about gene editing.

It really shouldn’t have come as a surprise. Perhaps no technology has more power to transform medicine, and its vast potential is just beginning to be realized. Gene editing can be used to delete, insert, or alter portions of our genetic code. We’ve been able to modify DNA for years, but newer technologies like CRISPR mean that we can do it faster, more accurately, and more efficiently than ever before. In 2023, we saw the first approval of a CRISPR-based gene-editing therapy. And many more are to come. **So let’s take a look at the developments that made news this year. What is the promise of gene editing, and what are the current pitfalls?**

### **Lucky breaks and next steps**

Casgevy, the first CRISPR therapy, has already been approved in the UK and US to treat sickle-cell disease. And it’s now [on the cusp of approval in the European Union](https://sicklecellanemianews.com/news/casgevy-sickle-cell-gene-edited-therapy-favored-eu-approval/). Sickle-cell disease is caused by a mutation in the hemoglobin gene that leads to a characteristic crescent moon shape of the red blood cells. The treatment doesn’t address the underlying cause of the disease; instead, it disables another gene, one that hampers production of a type of hemoglobin that people normally produce only in the womb and as babies. With that gene out of commission, production of this second type of hemoglobin resumes. The therapy works because cells with fetal hemoglobin won’t form sickles. You can read more about the fascinating backstory on the development of Casgevy in [this story](https://www.technologyreview.com/2023/12/07/1084629/lucky-break-crispr-vertex/) by my colleague Antonio Regalado.

Why go at it in this roundabout way? Current versions of CRISPR work best as a pair of scissors, creating snips that disable genes. That limits its usefulness. **New versions of CRISPR will allow researchers to alter the genetic code or even insert new genes, which will make it possible to address a wide variety of genetic diseases.**

Verve Therapeutics, for example, is testing an approach called base editing. Jessica Hamzelou [covered this technique in depth in this story](https://www.technologyreview.com/2023/01/19/1067074/next-for-crispr/) in January: “There are four DNA bases: A, T, C, and G. Instead of cutting the DNA, CRISPR 2.0 machinery can convert one base letter into another. Base editing can swap a C for a T, or an A for a G.” According to Kiran Musunuru, cofounder and senior scientific advisor at Verve, “It’s no longer acting like scissors, but more like a pencil and eraser.”

Verve’s therapy, now being tested in a small clinical trial, swaps out a single base in a gene for a protein called PCSK9, which is linked to high cholesterol. (The therapy was [one of MIT Technology Review’s 10 Breakthrough Technologies 2023](https://www.technologyreview.com/2023/01/09/1064857/crispr-high-cholesterol-10-breakthrough-technologies-2023/).) That change disables the gene, which means that the body makes less PCSK9 and cholesterol levels fall. In November the company [announced interim results](https://ir.vervetx.com/news-releases/news-release-details/verve-therapeutics-announces-interim-data-verve-101): a single injection of the therapy reduced LDL levels in the blood by up to 55% in 10 people with a genetic condition that causes high cholesterol.

CRISPR 3.0, which allows scientists to replace bits of DNA or insert new chunks of genetic code, is still being tested in animals. One company, Prime Medicine, plans to seek FDA approval to launch a human trial of a treatment for chronic granulomatous disease, a genetic immune disorder, in 2024 .

### **Pitfalls remain, at least for now.**

The only approved CRISPR therapy isn’t a simple fix. Patients have to undergo a bone-marrow transplant: after chemotherapy to destroy their faulty cells, stem cells are extracted, edited in the lab, and then reinfused. Jimi Olaghere, one of the few people to have received the therapy, [wrote about how arduous this was](https://www.technologyreview.com/2023/12/04/1084209/vertex-exacel-approval-gene-editing-sickle-cell-disease-patient/). The cell collection process left him so weak he needed blood transfusions. And the chemo meant “dealing with nausea, weakness, hair loss, debilitating mouth sores, and the risk of exacerbating the underlying condition.” All told, he spent 17 weeks in the hospital.

Given the complexity of the treatment, you won’t be surprised to learn that it’s expensive—it costs an estimated $2.2 million. That price tag means it’s out of reach for many, especially people in low-income countries.

**Vertex is already exploring strategies to make sickle-cell therapies more accessible and affordable.** Antonio spoke to the company’s head of research, David Altshuler, about some of the strategies [earlier this month](https://www.technologyreview.com/2023/12/15/1085380/vertex-sickle-cell-pill-treatment/).  One of the more promising approaches might not involve gene editing at all.

## Related Story

Thanks for the cure, CRISPR. Now companies want a pill to treat sickle-cell disease instead.

“One question I get a lot is: How are we going to get to the rest of the world?” Altshuler said. “And I think the answer is not by trying to do bone-marrow transplants in the rest of the world. It’s just too resource intensive, and the infrastructure is not there. I think the goal will be achieved sooner by finding another modality, like a pill that can be distributed much more effectively.”

Safety concerns abound.Gene editing is permanent, and one of the biggest concerns is that these therapies might miss the mark and create “off-target” effects. Regulators were so concerned about this possibility that [an FDA advisory committee met in November](https://www.reuters.com/business/healthcare-pharmaceuticals/us-fda-advisers-consider-need-more-studies-vertexcrispr-gene-therapy-2023-10-27/) to assess whether Vertex would need to provide additional data to prove Casgevy’s safety. (They ultimately decided the existing data was sufficient for approval.) The company plans to follow up with patients for 15 years to confirm safety.

Most experts think base editing, which doesn’t involve snipping, should be safer than the CRISPR scissors. But even there, safety has been front and center in discussions. The positive results in Verve’s trial of base editing to treat high cholesterol were partly overshadowed by the fact that [one participant had a heart attack, and another died of cardiac arrest.](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-023-03543-z)

**The epic patent dispute over CRISPR has long been another** [**potential hiccup**](https://www.technologyreview.com/2023/12/01/1084152/the-first-crispr-cure-might-kickstart-the-next-big-patent-battle/) **for possible therapies.** But this month [Antonio reported on a partial resolution](https://www.technologyreview.com/2023/12/13/1085209/vertex-license-controversial-crispr-patent-editas/). Vertex agreed to pay tens of millions of dollars to competitor Editas and the Broad Institute for the right to use Broad’s CRISPR patent, thus avoiding a potential lawsuit. “It’s not yet clear if the license agreement points to an end of the fierce patent fight between Broad and Berkeley. That has been continuing before a US patent court, with Berkeley still trying to overturn its rival’s claims,” he wrote.

**Despite the pitfalls, it’s clear that gene-editing therapies, when they work, can be transformational.** Olaghere [detailed his experience as a trial participant](https://www.technologyreview.com/2023/12/04/1084209/vertex-exacel-approval-gene-editing-sickle-cell-disease-patient/).  “I started to experience things I had only dreamed of: boundless energy and the ability to recover by merely sleeping. My physical symptoms—including a yellowish tint in my eyes caused by the rapid breakdown of malfunctioning red blood cells—virtually disappeared overnight,” he wrote. “Most significantly, I gained the confidence that sickle-cell disease won’t take me away from my family, and a sense of control over my own destiny.”

--- SOURCE 2: Future of CRISPR: Tech Heralds Landmark Clinical Trials ---
URL: https://www.endocrinologyadvisor.com/features/future-of-crispr/

SUMMARY:
In the acute and critical care arena, advancements in gene editing for cancer have been among the most important gene editing applications.22 Following positive early-phase outcomes and improved manufacturing techniques that enhance CAR T cell yields, CRISPR Therapeutics has progressed to phase 1/2 trials (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifiers: NCT05643742, NCT05795595) with their advanced CAR T cell therapies targeting CD70 and CD19 proteins, which are prevalent on various tumor and blood cancer cells.23 These updated therapies now feature additional edits to Regnase-1 and _TGFBR-2_ genes to enhance T cell anti-cancer activity by deactivating genes that inhibit T cell functions.

Future of CRISPR: Gene Editing Technologies Herald Landmark Clinical Trials
===========================================================================

[Jennifer Leavitt, MS](https://www.endocrinologyadvisor.com/author/jennifer-leavitt-ms)

|

Publish Date August 9, 2024 

**Contents**[hide](https://www.endocrinologyadvisor.com/features/future-of-crispr/#)

[1 Blood Diseases](https://www.endocrinologyadvisor.com/features/future-of-crispr/#Blood_Diseases)

[2 Urinary Tract Infections](https://www.endocrinologyadvisor.com/features/future-of-crispr/#Urinary_Tract_Infections)

[3 Hereditary Transthyretin Amyloidosis](https://www.endocrinologyadvisor.com/features/future-of-crispr/#Hereditary_Transthyretin_Amyloidosis)

[4 Hereditary Angioedema](https://www.endocrinologyadvisor.com/features/future-of-crispr/#Hereditary_Angioedema)

[5 Cardiovascular Disease](https://www.endocrinologyadvisor.com/features/future-of-crispr/#Cardiovascular_Disease)

[6 Type 1 Diabetes](https://www.endocrinologyadvisor.com/features/future-of-crispr/#Type_1_Diabetes)

[7 Systemic Lupus Erythematosus](https://www.endocrinologyadvisor.com/features/future-of-crispr/#Systemic_Lupus_Erythematosus)

[8 HIV](https://www.endocrinologyadvisor.com/features/future-of-crispr/#HIV)

[9 Blood Cancers](https://www.endocrinologyadvisor.com/features/future-of-crispr/#Blood_Cancers)

[10 Challenges](https://www.endocrinologyadvisor.com/features/future-of-crispr/#Challenges)

Clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR), building on earlier-developed techniques such as zinc finger nucleases (ZFNs) and transcription activator-like effector nucleases (TALENs), has emerged as the most frequently used method of genome editing, with many new studies and trials gaining traction.1

CRISPR technology includes a variety of methods, each suitable for different applications.2 CRISPR-Cas9, derived from the immune system of _Streptococcus pyogenes_ bacteria, is the most widely used CRISPR-associated protein to date. Functioning as molecular scissors, CRISPR-Cas9 cuts DNA at specific sites, led by a programmable guide RNA that targets specific genetic sequences for disruption or correction.

CRISPR-Cas12, previously known as Cpf1, is another commonly-used variant. It provides certain advantages over Cas9, such as requiring a shorter guide RNA and producing a staggered cut, making it useful in diagnostic applications. CRISPR-Cas3, useful for extensive removal of DNA, is used to eliminate viral genomes from infected cells.

A relatively new addition to the research front, CRISPR-Cas13 targets RNA rather than DNA and is being explored for applications such as RNA degradation in influenza and SARS-CoV-2. Base editors and prime editors — derivatives of CRISPR technology — enable direct, single-nucleotide edits without introducing double-stranded breaks, which minimizes unintended effects.

Late 2023 saw a significant milestone in genome editing with the Food and Drug Administration (FDA)’s first approval of a CRISPR application for clinical use.3 The CRISPR-Cas9 drug, Casgevy® (exagamglogene autotemcel or exa-cel), is indicated for treatment of both sickle cell disease (SCD) and transfusion-dependent beta thalassemia (TDT).

Blood Diseases
--------------

Developed by Vertex Pharmaceuticals and CRISPR Therapeutics, exa-cel was approved on the basis of phase 3 trial data (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT03745287](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT03745287)) and functions by editing a gene to reactivate fetal hemoglobin production.4 This action alleviates the defective red blood cells responsible for SCD. Sixteen of 17 patients in the study stopped experiencing vaso-occlusive crises. Among the 27 patients with TDT, 25 were no longer dependent on transfusions. In some cases, these results were observed for more than 3 years.5

Although the mutations causing SCD and TDT are not directly corrected, the treatment effectively manages the disease long term. Chemotherapy is used before treatment to prepare for the integration of genetically modified cells, as exa-cel involves ex vivo editing techniques. CRISPR Therapeutics is exploring the potential for in vivo editing, which could reduce or eliminate the need for intensive chemotherapy and lower overall chemotherapy doses.

Other researchers are also examining CRISPR for SCD. Editas Medicine is conducting phase 1/2 trials (RUBY; ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT04853576](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04853576)) using Cas12a to activate fetal hemoglobin production, showing notable clinical benefits without serious adverse events.6 Beam Therapeutics has initiated a phase 1/2 trial (BEACON; ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT05456880](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05456880)) using a base editing technique to enhance fetal hemoglobin without causing double-stranded DNA breaks, potentially minimizing safety risks.5 A University of California Consortium-led non-profit initiative is addressing direct repair of the SCD mutation, though it faces manufacturing delays.7

Urinary Tract Infections
------------------------

Beyond blood diseases, CRISPR applications extend to treating common yet challenging conditions such as urinary tract infections (UTIs), which can recur frequently and become antibiotic resistant. Locus Biosciences is recruiting approximately 800 participants for a phase 2/3 trial (ELIMINATE; ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT05488340](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05488340)) to address UTIs caused by antimicrobial resistant _Escherichia coli_.8

LBP-EC01, a cocktail of 3 bacteriophages with CRISPR-Cas3, targets and destroys the genomes of the most common _E coli_ strains, responsible for approximately 95% of UTIs. This therapy, which degrades DNA rather than repairing it, was first tested in a US-based phase 1b trial (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT04191148](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04191148)) completed in February 2021 — the first to apply CRISPR technology in treating bacterial infections.9

Hereditary Transthyretin Amyloidosis
------------------------------------

CRISPR technology is also making strides in tackling complex genetic diseases. The first CRISPR-Cas9 therapy administered in a lipid nanoparticle (LNP) for systemic delivery was NTLA-2001, developed by Intellia Therapeutics to treat hereditary transthyretin amyloidosis (hATTR), a fatal, genetic protein-folding disease caused by mutations in the TTR gene that leads to production of a malfunctioning protein.10 Lipid nanoparticles tend to accumulate in the liver, where most TTR protein synthesis also occurs, placing drug and target in close proximity. The drug is administered via a single IV dose.

Clinical trials for NTLA-2001 began in late 2020, focusing on 2 patient groups: those with symptoms of neuropathy 10 and those with cardiomyopathy (ClinicialTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT04601051](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04601051)).11 The studies showed a significant reduction in toxic protein levels — over 85% even at the lowest dose, and more than 90% among patients receiving the highest dose. This reduction directly impacts the progression of hATTR, as lower TTR levels correlate with reduced amyloid deposits and, consequently, slower disease progression.

Researchers are adding a mid-range dose for further investigation. Intellia is recruiting for a phase 3 double-blind, placebo-controlled trial (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT06128629](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT06128629)) to assess NTLA-2001 for cardiomyopathy.12

Hereditary Angioedema
---------------------

Intellia is also advancing a second CRISPR-Cas9 drug delivered through lipid nanoparticles, NTLA-2002, to address hereditary angioedema (HAE).13 HAE is marked by recurrent, severe swelling attacks that can be life-threatening, especially when the airways are involved. The therapy targets the _KLKB1_ gene, which plays a role in the production of bradykinin, a peptide that promotes the characteristic inflammation and vascular permeability of HAE.

The treatment is delivered via a 1-time intravenous administration,13 and clinical trials (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT05120830](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05120830)) are evaluating 3 different dosage levels among 10 participants. Early results have shown a significant decrease in the frequency of attacks, with the highest dosage achieving more than a 90% reduction in the inflammatory protein levels.

Intellia is moving the therapy into a global phase 3 trial in 2024 to confirm the results on a broader scale and assess the treatment’s impact across diverse populations.14

Cardiovascular Disease
----------------------

The results seen in hereditary angioedema may lead to a broader application of CRISPR in other serious conditions. Trials for cardiovascular diseases, type 1 diabetes, cancers, autoimmune diseases, and HIV are already underway, in earlier phases.

In one instance, a gene editing treatment for familial hypercholesterolemia, developed by Verve Therapeutics, is progressing through a phase 1 trial (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT05398029](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05398029?a=1)) to address high cholesterol levels.15 The drug, Verve-101, uses base editing delivered through lipid nanoparticles to modify the _PCSK9_ gene in the liver, aiming to reduce cholesterol without the double-stranded breaks typically associated with CRISPR-Cas9.

Early results have shown a significant reduction in low-density lipoprotein cholesterol, exceeding traditional statin effects. Investigators have reported significant cardiovascular events among participants, prompting further data requirements. A phase 2 trial for either Verve-101 or Verve-102, which uses an identical gene editing mechanism but different lipid nanoparticle delivery system, is planned for 2025.16

Simultaneously, CRISPR Therapeutics has begun phase 1 trials targeting angiopoietin-like 3 protein and lipoprotein(a), which are linked to cardiovascular risks.17 These trials also use lipid nanoparticles and focus on more prevalent genetic variants, potentially broadening applications in heart health.

Type 1 Diabetes
---------------

CRISPR also presents possibilities for the management of chronic metabolic diseases such as type 1 diabetes. In partnership with ViaCyte, who is no longer involved in the study,18 CRISPR Therapeutics initiated a phase 1 trial (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT05565248](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05565248)) targeting type 1 diabetes.19 The trial employs CRISPR-Cas9 gene-editing to modify pancreatic cells derived from stem cells.

“

As we traverse the vistas of continued advancements, converge in interdisciplinary collaborations, and orchestrate the confluence of nanomaterial integration, a vibrant symphony reverberates, a cadence that heralds transformative discoveries, novel therapies, and a reverberating symphony of improved human health outcomes.

The key modification involves editing immune-related genes and placing them in a specialized pouch within the patient, making them less visible to the immune system. The treatment is designed to support nutrient and insulin exchange while reducing immune system attack, potentially eliminating the need for lifelong immunosuppression.

This approach may diffuse the many challenges associated with traditional pancreatic cell transplants, such as the risks for long-term immunosuppression and incompatibility of donor cells.19

Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
----------------------------

Addressing another chronic disease, CRISPR Therapeutics is also conducting a pre-clinical trial study using next-generation CD19-targeting chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cells for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).20 This marks the first use of CRISPR gene-editing technology for autoimmune diseases, specifically adapting CAR T cells to target and temporarily deplete B cells.

Investigators face a major challenge as they attempt to alleviate SLE through B cell depletion without causing prolonged immunodeficiency or exacerbating the autoimmune condition. Unlike cancer treatments in which long-term CAR T cell persistence is critical, this approach for SLE transiently resets the immune system, potentially inducing remission without the need for continuous presence of these modified cells.

HIV
---

Although HIV has become a chronic disease for many, CRISPR applications targeting HIV will pave the way for genome editing treatments for viral diseases. One new approach directly targets HIV DNA embedded in a host’s genome.21 Excision Biotherapeutics is conducting a phase 1 clinical trial (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT05144386](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05144386)) for an HIV treatment that uses CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing technology.

In this process, guide RNAs direct the Cas9 enzyme to precisely excise key segments of the viral genome at 2 specific sites, aimed at completely removing the virus from infected cells. The CRISPR components are delivered via an adeno-associated virus 9 (AAV9) vector in a single infusion.

Initiated in July 2022, the trial has thus far involved dosing a small number of participants, with plans to increase the dosage contingent on initial safety results.21 This trial is among the first to test the potential of CRISPR technology for treating chronic viral infections.

Blood Cancers
-------------

In the acute and critical care arena, advancements in gene editing for cancer have been among the most important gene editing applications.22 Following positive early-phase outcomes and improved manufacturing techniques that enhance CAR T cell yields, CRISPR Therapeutics has progressed to phase 1/2 trials (ClinicalTrials.gov Identifiers: [NCT05643742](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05643742), [NCT05795595](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05795595)) with their advanced CAR T cell therapies targeting CD70 and CD19 proteins, which are prevalent on various tumor and blood cancer cells.23 These updated therapies now feature additional edits to Regnase-1 and _TGFBR-2_ genes to enhance T cell anti-cancer activity by deactivating genes that inhibit T cell functions.

Caribou Biosciences has also seen success with its CAR T cells that target CD19, incorporating a PD-1 knockout to circumvent cancer cell immune evasion.24 Promising results from its phase 1 trial (ANTLER; ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: [NCT04637763](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04637763)) showed sustained remission among a significant number of participants, prompting Caribou to prepare for a direct transition from phase 1 to phase 3 trials, aimed at combating aggressive non-Hodgkin lymphomas. CAR T therapies typically use CRISPR-Cas9.

Challenges
----------

Financially, Casgevy’s $2 million per-patient price tag may be met with resistance by insurance companies, and future drugs will likely be just as expensive.25.26 Formulary concerns will prompt robust cost-benefit analyses of these expenses against potential long-term savings from reduced hospital visits and chronic care.

At the same time, regulatory bodies are struggling to keep pace with rapid advancements in gene therapy.25 Agencies will have to establish standards to manage the risks for potential off-target effects and other unintended genetic modifications. The ethical implications of gene editing, especially concerning edits that could be passed to future generations, require careful consideration and oversight.

As research continues, the focus will also be on rising to the challenges of affordability and regulatory compliance so that gene editing can be both effective and accessible across scientific and medical communities.

Aljabali and colleagues 25 note in their work on CRISPR-Cas9 technology, “As we traverse the vistas of continued advancements, converge in interdisciplinary collaborations, and orchestrate the confluence of nanomaterial integration, a vibrant symphony reverberates, a cadence that heralds transformative discoveries, novel therapies, and a reverberating symphony of improved human health outcomes.”

The advent of gene-editing technologies, particularly CRISPR-based systems, has revolutionized the landscape of biomedical research and gene therapy. Ongoing research in gene editing has led to the rapid iteration of CRISPR technologies, such as base and prime editors, enabling precise nucleotide changes without the need for generating harmful double-strand breaks (DSBs). Furthermore, innovations such as CRISPR fusion systems with DNA recombinases, DNA polymerases, and DNA ligases have expanded the size limitations for edited sequences, opening new avenues for therapeutic development. Beyond the CRISPR system, mobile genetic elements (MGEs) and epigenetic editors are emerging as efficient alternatives for precise large insertions or stable gene manipulation in mammalian cells. These advances collectively set the stage for next-generation gene therapy development. This review highlights recent developments of genetic and epigenetic editing tools and explores preclinical innovations poised to advance the field.

Introduction
------------

In bacteria, the clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR) system uses RNA as a guide to direct CRISPR-associated protein 9 (Cas9) nuclease to cut invading phage DNA.[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib1) About a decade ago, researchers realized the potential of this system as a simple and robust gene-editing tool.[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib2),[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib3) Compared to earlier generations of gene-editing tools like zinc-finger nucleases (ZFNs) and transcriptional activator-like effector nucleases (TALENs), which require sophisticated protein design for every target, the CRISPR system offers unparalleled programmability. The US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has approved the first CRISPR-Cas9-based gene-editing therapy, Exa-cel (exagamglogene autotemcel, brand name Casgevy), for human sickle cell disease (SCD). This milestone marks the official entry of gene therapy into the CRISPR-Cas9 era.

Despite emerging as the leading gene-editing tool, gene-editing therapies still face significant challenges due to the inherent characteristics of the CRISPR technologies, such as nuclease size, protospacer-adjacent motif (PAM) site requirements, off-target effects, editing efficiency, genotoxicity and immunotoxicity. Fortunately, the scientific community’s dedication to democratizing the CRISPR platform has led to a rapid and substantial refinements and expansions of the editing technologies over the past decade. These technological advancements provide an unprecedented opportunity to develop innovative gene therapies for many human diseases. In this review, we focus on recent advancements in gene-editing technologies and their applications in preclinical and clinical settings.

Early generations of gene editors
---------------------------------

ZFNs are assemblies of several zinc-finger repeats fused with an endonuclease domain. Each zinc-finger module recognizes a specific DNA triplet, and multiple zinc-finger repeats can be combined to precisely target specific genomic sequences. In contrast, the transcription activator-like effector (TALE) was found to bind to a single DNA base, thereby improving the targeting accuracy and simplifying design. Dimerization of paired ZFNs or TALENs is required for the generation of DSBs to initiate DNA repair through non-homologous DNA end joining (NHEJ) or homology-directed recombination (HDR).

Nevertheless, DNA sequence specificity depends on these DNA-recognizing modules, requiring laborious engineering and customization, not to mention the lengthy validation and production process for each different target gene. Despite the early resolution of the zinc-finger crystal structure in the 1990s,[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib4),[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib5) it took nearly two decades of research and optimization to advance ZFN technology to the stage of clinical trials ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#fig1)).

Created with [BioRender.com](http://biorender.com/).

Although ZFNs and TALENs were swiftly superseded by CRISPR, their medical achievements remain invaluable, laying a crucial foundation for the rapid advancement of modern genome-editing technologies. For instance, SB-728, a zinc-finger-based therapeutics developed to confer HIV resistance by disrupting the viral receptor CCR5 on patient CD4 T cells, is the first gene-editing treatment advanced to clinical trials.[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib6) Likewise, TALEN technology was employed in Cellectis’s UCART19 T cell therapy to eliminate TCR-α chain (TRAC) and CD52, reducing the risk of graft-versus-host diseases (GvHDs) during treatment of B cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL).[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib7)

The CRISPR-CAS9 system
----------------------

Cas9 proteins from type-II CRISPR systems are RNA-guided nucleases originally discovered in bacteria that generate DSBs in target DNA sequences to specifically inactivate invading phages.[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib8),[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib9) They are guided by CRISPR RNAs (crRNAs), associated with _trans_-activating crRNAs (tracrRNAs) to facilitate the formation of ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complex. Cas9 can be reprogrammed for precise genome editing in mammalian cells by using a single guide RNA (sgRNA), which fuses crRNA and tracrRNA to target a specific DNA sequence.[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib2),[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib3) The Cas9-gRNA RNP complex first binds to the cognate PAM sequence, then unwinds the double-stranded DNA and forms an RNA-DNA heteroduplex between the sgRNA spacer and the target DNA strand, displacing the non-target DNA strand to form a single-stranded DNA R-loop.[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib10) Once the sgRNA spacer matches the target sequence, Cas9 undergoes a conformational change and introduces a DSB approximately 3–4 nucleotides upstream of the PAM sequence. The resulting break is then repaired by either error-prone NHEJ or the high-fidelity HDR pathway. This has been extensively reviewed by others.[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib11),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib12),[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib13)

The RNA-guided CRISPR system substantially improves the specificity of gene editing compared to earlier technologies like ZFNs and TALENs.[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib14) In addition to its precision, the simplicity of gRNA design and rapid turnaround time enable high-throughput screening, allowing for efficient identification of genes associated with specific phenotypes. Furthermore, CRISPR’s versatility extends beyond protein-coding genes to target noncoding region such as promoters,[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib15),[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib16) enhancers,[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib17) splicing junctions,[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib18) and noncoding RNAs,[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib19) broadening its applications in both fundamental research and therapeutic development.

The most widely used _Streptococcus pyogenes_ Cas9 (SpCas9) is composed of 1,368 amino acids, recognizing an NGG PAM and displaying robust gene-editing activity. Many other Cas9 orthologs were discovered, including those from _Staphylococcus aureus_ Cas9 (SaCas9),[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib20)_Neisseria meningitidis_ Cas9 (NmeCas9),[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib21),[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib22),[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib23)_Campylobacter jejuni_ Cas9 (CjCas9),[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib24) among others (see [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#tbl1)). These Cas9 variants differ in their size, cognate PAM sequence, sgRNA scaffold, optimal spacer length, editing efficiency, and specificity. The smaller-sized Cas9s such as SaCas9, Nme2Cas9, and CjCas9 hold a great advantage for _in vivo_ applications using adeno-associated virus (AAV) vectors that have a limited cargo capacity. However, as compared to SpCas9, the small Cas9 variants generally have a lower efficiency. Recently, an online platform called DeepSmallCas9 was developed to predict the best-performing small Cas9 based on its activities across thousands of target sequences utilizing high-throughput methods and computational modeling.[57](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib57)

The scope of CRISPR gene editing is limited by a PAM sequence within the target sequence. Some naturally existing Cas9 recognizes a simpler PAM. For example, Cas9 from _Streptococcus canis_ (ScCas9) recognizes an NNG PAM.[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib36) Rational engineering and directed evolution approaches have been used to evolve SpCas9 to recognize simpler PAMs, such as SpCas9-NG,[58](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib58) xCas9-3.7,[59](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib59) and even PAMless Cas9-SpRY.[60](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib60) The smaller Cas9 orthologs tend to use more complex PAM sequences, with a few exceptions, such as SauriCas9 and SlugCas9, which can recognize NNGG PAM.[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib30),[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib31) Rational engineering and phage-assisted continuous evolution (PACE) were also applied to engineer smaller Cas9 proteins with relaxed PAM targeting ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#tbl1)). For example, SaCas9-KKH was relaxed to recognize NNNRRT instead of NNGRRT by the parental SaCas9,[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib27) Nme2Cas9 was engineered to target single-nucleotide-pyrimidine PAM (N4CN or N4TN),[61](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib61) SlugCas9-NNG was modified to recognize NNG PAM,[62](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib62) and evoCjCas9 was evolved to target N4AH and N5HA PAM.[63](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib63) These efforts have greatly broadened the targeting scope.

Although highly sequence specific, Cas9 can tolerate one or a few nucleobase mismatches of the targeting sgRNAs in the seed region (complementary to target sequence), leading to substantial off-target editing activities.[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib11),[64](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib64),[65](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib65),[66](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib66),[67](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib67) Various computational platforms and experimental approaches have been developed to investigate the off-target activities of Cas9 and optimize sgRNA performance.[68](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib68),[69](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib69),[70](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib70),[71](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib71),[72](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib72),[73](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib73),[74](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib74) CRISPick and Cas-Designer are the most widely used online tools for sgRNA design and CRISPR analysis.[75](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib75),[76](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib76) Recently, CRISPR off-target (CRISOT) was developed based on molecular dynamics (MD) of RNA-DNA interaction fingerprint, demonstrating a generalizable framework for off-target prediction without extensive experimental fine-tuning.[70](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib70) Computational methods such as neural and deep-learning networks have been utilized to establish an off-target model based on high-throughput detection omics data,[72](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib72),[77](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib77),[78](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib78),[79](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib79) yielding better performance than previous versions. These insights have been instrumental in enhancing the stringency of the CRISPR system. By rational protein design and directed evolution, scientists have engineered Cas9 variants with increased fidelity, such as eSpCas9(1.1) and SpCas9-HF1 ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#tbl1)).[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib25),[80](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib80) However, these high-fidelity variants often have lower on-target efficiency.[81](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib81) This reduced on-target activity become more pronounced when combined with the PAM-relaxed variants, limiting their practical usage.[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib11),[82](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib82)

In addition to Cas9 protein engineering, other approaches have been taken to reduce off-target activities. One strategy utilizes adjacently paired sgRNAs with Cas9 nickase to induce a DSB.[83](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib83) Interestingly, DNA nicking also promotes high-fidelity HDR repair rate.[84](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S152500162500200X#bib84),[85](https

enriched_points.expanded_content: The CRISPR technology landscape continues to advance, with innovations like base and prime editing improving precision and efficiency, enabling targeted therapies for a broader range of genetic disorders.

Artificial intelligence is revolutionizing gene therapy research by optimizing gene editing designs and predicting off-target effects, significantly enhancing the safety and efficacy of therapeutic applications.
Gene therapy is moving towards personalized approaches, allowing tailored treatments based on individual genetic profiles, thus improving outcomes for patients with unique genetic conditions.
Innovative delivery systems, like lipid nanoparticles and viral vectors, are being developed to enhance the efficiency of gene editing, ensuring precise targeting and reduced immune responses.

The scope of gene therapy is expanding into various fields, including cancer, metabolic disorders, and infectious diseases, promising transformative treatments that address previously untreatable conditions.
